perturbation.original_text: 10. If disclosure (transfer, announcement) of Confident ial Information is required from the Receiving Party and/or its representatives by a court or anot her governmental body, the Receiving Party shall promptly, but in any event before the disclos ure (or transfer/announcement) of the Confidential Information, notify the Disclosing Par ty in writing and provide the Disclosing Party with the opportunity to take appropriate measures t o protect and preserve confidentiality of such information. If such a request (or demand) from a c ourt or another governmental body to disclose (or transfer/announce) Confidential Information has not been revoked (or invalidated) before the moment the relevant information is to be provided, the Receiving Party has the right to disclose (or transfer) only the part of the Confidential Inf ormation that it is obliged to disclose (or transfe r) under the laws of Ukraine
perturbation.changed_text: 10. If disclosure (transfer, announcement) of Confidential Information is required from the Receiving Party and/or its representatives by a court or another governmental body, the Receiving Party shall disclose (or transfer) the Confidential Information that it is obliged to disclose (or transfer) under the laws of Ukraine.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information under court order. This directly removes a legal protection. In Ukraine, failure to comply with a court order to disclose information can result in penalties; however, this does not supersede the Disclosing Party's right to take actions to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Law of Ukraine on Information, Article 21
perturbation.law_citation: Article 21 of the Law of Ukraine on Information
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 21 of the Law of Ukraine on Information ensures protection of confidential information and allows for legal measures to prevent unauthorized disclosure. By removing the notification requirement, the modified text potentially violates the Disclosing Party's right to protect their confidential information as ensured under Ukrainian law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information under court order. This creates a legal contradiction because Article 21 of the Law of Ukraine on Information ensures protection of confidential information and allows for legal measures to prevent unauthorized disclosure, as stated in the Law Explanation. By removing the notification requirement, the modified text potentially violates the Disclosing Party's right to protect their confidential information. The scraped snippet, although primarily a link to the law, confirms the existence of the 'Law of Ukraine on Information', supporting the Law Explanation. This removal of a protection directly contradicts the purpose of ensuring confidential information is protected under Ukrainian law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the preservation and nondisclosure of the Confidential Information under this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect without being limited in time, except for cases when the Disclosi ng Party provides its written consent as to any reduction of said term.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the preservation and nondisclosure of the Confidential Information under this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for three years, unless otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the confidentiality period to three years, removing the perpetual obligation and the explicit requirement for the Disclosing Party's written consent for any reduction of this term. This can cause the contract to violate trade secret laws in jurisdictions such as the United States.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), adopted in many U.S. states, protects trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. By limiting the confidentiality period to three years, the modified text could fail to adequately protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under the UTSA, potentially leading to legal challenges if the Receiving Party discloses such information after the three-year period, even if it still qualifies as a trade secret.

perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures perpetual confidentiality unless explicitly waived by the Disclosing Party. The changed text limits this confidentiality to three years. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the UTSA because it may not adequately protect trade secrets, especially if they remain confidential and valuable beyond the three-year limit. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant (it only provides a search interface), the Perturbation Explanation confirms this time limitation creates a legal loophole that could undermine trade secret protections. By removing the perpetual obligation, the modified text directly contradicts the aim to protect trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage.

perturbation.original_text: 13. In case the Receiving Party breaches this Agreement , the Disclosing Party, the Target or any of their Affiliates are entitled to receive from the R eceiving Party compensation of financial and moral damages, suffered by the Disclosing Party, th e Target or any of their Affiliates respectively, as a result of such breach by the Rec eiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. In case the Receiving Party breaches this Agreement, the Disclosing Party is entitled to receive from the Receiving Party compensation of financial damages, as a result of such breach by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the entitlement to compensation for 'moral damages' and limits who can claim damages to only the 'Disclosing Party'. This creates an omission that contradicts laws allowing for broader damage claims and remedies for related parties affected by a breach, which are often legally protected.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 23 of the Civil Code of Ukraine
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 23 of the Civil Code of Ukraine provides for compensation for both property and non-property (moral) damages. The modified text omits the right to claim moral damages, thereby contradicting Ukrainian law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Code of Ukraine - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Civil code adopted in 2003 This article includes a list of general references , but it lacks sufficient corresponding inline citations . Please help to improve this article by introducing more precise citations. ( July 2020 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) The Civil Code of Ukraine ( Ukrainian : Цивільний кодекс України , Tsyvilnyi Kodeks Ukrayiny ) is a Ukrainian codification of private law ( civil code ), basic normative legal act. It regulates personal non-property and property relations (civil relations), based on legal equality, free will, property independence of their participants. It was adopted by the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine on 16 January 2003. Prior to the adoption of the Civil Code of Ukraine, the Civil Code of the Ukrainian SSR dated 18 July 1963 was in force on the territory of Ukraine (insofar as it didn't contradict the legislation of Ukraine adopted after the proclamation of Ukraine's independence after 24 August 1991). Following dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991, Ukraine has shifted from the system of socialist law to civil law . History [ edit ] The objective necessity of adopting a new civil code arose immediately after the proclamation of independence and the reorientation of economic development to a market path. At the same time, due to the difficult economic situation, many provisions of the Civil Code of the Ukrainian SSR, which contained provisions with the expression of amounts in rubles, lost their significance. To ensure the development of new economic relations, a number of laws were adopted, in particular “On Property”, “On Entrepreneurship”, “On Business Societies”. The development of the code has been carried out for over 12 years, however, until the adoption of the final version among the legal community of Ukraine, no consensus was reached on certain provisions of the code. In particular, there was no consensus on the question of the existence of collective property, the independence of family law and private international law. As a result, the book "Family Law", which formed the basis of the Family Code of Ukraine, was removed from the Civil Code. Also later, a separate Law of Ukraine "On Private International Law" was adopted. After adoption, the code was not signed by the President of Ukraine (the veto was applied, the act was returned to parliament with a number of proposals), but the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine overcame the President's veto by its decision. The main remarks of the President, which have not lost their relevance today, boiled down to contradictions between the Civil Code and the Economic Code of Ukraine adopted on the same day. The codes used different techniques for constructing legal structures and different terminology, but at the same time a number of social relations were simultaneously regulated by two codes. The structure of the Civil Code [ edit ] Built on the pandect system , the Civil Code of Ukraine consists of six books, delimited by scope: The Book One. General Provisions Chapter 1. Basic Provisions Chapter 2. Persons Chapter 3. Objects of Civil Rights Chapter 4. Transactions. Representation Chapter 5. Terms and Conditions . Statute of Limitations The Book Two. Personal Intangible Rights of an Individual The Book Three. Ownership and Other Property Rights Chapter 1. Ownership Chapter 2. Proprietary Rights to Another's Property The Book Four. Intellectual Property Rights The Book Five. Law of Obligations Chapter 1. General Provisions on Obligations Chapter 2. General Provisions on Contracts Chapter 3. Certain Types of Obligations Subsection 1. Contractual Obligations (Chapter 54-77) Subsection 2. Non-Contractual Obligations (Chapter 78-83) The Book Six. Inheritance Law There were 1308 articles in the code at the adoption time. Articles of the Code are divided into parts, which in turn can be subdivided into paragraphs. Scope of regulation [ edit ] In esse
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits compensation for 'moral damages' and limits who can claim damages to only the 'Disclosing Party'. The Law Explanation specifies that Article 23 of the Civil Code of Ukraine provides for compensation for both property and non-property (moral) damages. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Civil Code of Ukraine. The omission of 'moral damages' directly contradicts the provision for compensation for non-property damages under Ukrainian law, leading to a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12. All disputes which may arise from or in connection with this Agreement shall be settled through negotiations between the Parties. If such a dispute between the Parties cannot be settled through negotiations between the Parties, either Party may submit such a dispute to a court in accordance with [ specify the jurisdiction ] laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. All disputes which may arise from or in connection with this Agreement shall be settled through negotiations between the Parties. If such a dispute between the Parties cannot be settled through negotiations between the Parties, all claims are waived.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the right to pursue legal action in court. Many jurisdictions have laws preventing the complete waiver of certain legal rights, especially those related to fraud or intentional misconduct.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S. Code § 2
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act favors arbitration, but it does not allow for the complete waiver of all legal claims, particularly if fraud or duress is involved. Eliminating the right to court access, even with a negotiation clause, can be viewed as an unconscionable waiver of rights, conflicting with the FAA's principles of fairness and the ability to challenge arbitration outcomes under certain circumstances.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for disputes to be settled in court, while the changed text eliminates the right to pursue legal action in court, stating that 'all claims are waived' after negotiations fail. The Perturbation Explanation states that many jurisdictions prevent the complete waiver of certain legal rights. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippets discuss the Federal Arbitration Act, suggesting that while arbitration is favored, a complete waiver of legal claims, especially when fraud or duress is involved, may conflict with fairness principles. Although the snippets discuss arbitration, the FAA also speaks to grounds at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. Therefore, eliminating the right to court access creates a contradiction.
